title: Excess mortality: Raw number of deaths from all causes compared to projection based on previous years
subtitle: The reported number of weekly or monthly deaths in 2020–2024 and the projected number of deaths for 2020, which is based on the reported deaths in 2015–2019.
caption:
Excess mortality: Raw number of deaths from all causes compared to projection based on previous years

The reported number of weekly or monthly deaths in 2020–2024 and the projected number of deaths for 2020, which is based on the reported deaths in 2015–2019.

Weekly death counts in the United States from early 2020 through early 2021 show distinct deviations from historical baselines. The vertical axis measures the raw number of deaths ranging from 0 to 100,000, while the horizontal axis tracks time from January 5, 2020, to January 3, 2021. A black line labeled "Projected, 2020" establishes the expected mortality trend based on data from 2015 through 2019, hovering steadily between 50,000 and 60,000 deaths per week. Grey lines representing the individual years 2015 through 2019 cluster tightly around this projection, confirming a stable historical pattern. In contrast, the orange line for 2020 diverges significantly, rising above 60,000 in March, spiking to nearly 80,000 in late April, and surging again to exceed 85,000 by the start of 2021. The purple line for 2021 begins the period at the highest point on the chart, exceeding 85,000 deaths in early January before declining to join the baseline cluster by March. The teal line for 2022 and the blue line for 2023 remain largely within the historical range, tracking close to the projected values with only minor fluctuations. The data shows that reported figures might not count all deaths that occurred due to incomplete coverage and delays in reporting. The 2020 and early 2021 periods display mortality counts that consistently exceed the projected baseline and the range of previous years.
